# Voice Examples: Before & After Rewrites

Concrete examples showing how to transform generic writing into founder voice. Each example demonstrates one or more principles from the Founder Voice skill.

---

## Example 1: Section Opening (Dead Opener + Tone)

### Before (Generic)

> In this week's newsletter, we'll be taking a look at some of the latest developments in AI tooling for small businesses. There have been some exciting updates that we think you'll find valuable, and we're going to walk you through what they mean for your business.

### After (Founder Voice)

> AI tooling for small teams changed more in the last 30 days than in the previous six months. Three releases landed that actually matter — not the "we added AI to our settings page" kind, but the kind that replaces a full-time hire. Here is what shipped and why you should care.

### What Changed

- Eliminated the "in this week's newsletter" dead opener. Started with the strongest claim.
- Replaced vague "exciting updates" with a specific scope: "three releases."
- Swapped passive "we think you'll find valuable" with an opinionated take: "replaces a full-time hire."
- Cut word count from 52 to 46 while increasing information density.
- Added short-long rhythm: "Three releases landed that actually matter" (short) followed by a clarifying aside (long).

---

## Example 2: News Item (Practical Framing + Opinion Injection)

### Before (Generic)

> OpenAI recently announced updates to their API pricing structure. The new pricing model offers reduced costs for many use cases, which could be beneficial for developers and businesses that rely on these services. It's worth keeping an eye on how this develops.

### After (Founder Voice)

> OpenAI cut API prices by 40% on their mid-tier models last Tuesday. If you are spending more than $500/month on API calls, recalculate your unit economics this week — your margin on AI features just improved overnight. The catch: the price cut applies to newer model versions only, so teams pinned to older models get nothing. Here is why this matters for anyone building AI into their product: the cost floor for "good enough" AI just dropped below the threshold where it makes sense to build your own fine-tuned model. Stop debating build-vs-buy. At these prices, buy.

### What Changed

- Added specific numbers: "40%", "$500/month", "last Tuesday." Precision creates authority.
- Replaced "could be beneficial" with a direct instruction: "recalculate your unit economics this week."
- Named the trade-off: "the catch" paragraph acknowledges the limitation.
- Applied the "Here is why this matters" opinion injection framework.
- Ended with a clear, opinionated recommendation: "Stop debating build-vs-buy. At these prices, buy."
- Short-long sentence rhythm throughout. "Stop debating build-vs-buy." = 4 words. "At these prices, buy." = 4 words. Punchy close.

---

## Example 3: Feature/Tool Recommendation (Before-After Pattern)

### Before (Generic)

> There are many project management tools available on the market today. One solution that has been gaining traction is Linear, which offers a streamlined interface and robust feature set. Teams looking to improve their project management workflows may want to consider giving it a try.

### After (Founder Voice)

> We used Jira for two years. Every Monday started with 20 minutes of grooming tickets nobody read. Standups turned into status-update theater because the board was always out of date.

> We switched to Linear in January. Setup took an afternoon. Three weeks in, our sprint velocity tracking actually matched reality for the first time. The difference is not the features — Linear has fewer features than Jira. The difference is that Linear is fast enough that people actually use it. A tool your team ignores is not a tool. It is overhead.

### What Changed

- Replaced generic "many tools available" with a specific anecdote (Anecdote-Lesson pattern).
- Used Before-After comparison with mirrored specifics: "20 minutes of grooming" vs. "setup took an afternoon."
- Named both tools. No vague "a popular project management tool."
- Delivered an opinionated take: "A tool your team ignores is not a tool. It is overhead."
- Grounded the recommendation in a measurable outcome: "sprint velocity tracking actually matched reality."
- Final two sentences use short-short rhythm for emphasis.

---

## Example 4: Trend Analysis (Problem-Solution + Hedging Removal)

### Before (Generic)

> It seems like more and more companies are starting to explore the idea of async-first communication. Some people believe this could potentially lead to better productivity outcomes, though it arguably depends on the specific context and team dynamics. It might be worth considering whether this approach could work for your organization.

### After (Founder Voice)

> The async-first movement is not a trend. It is a correction. Synchronous communication dominated because offices demanded it, not because it was better. Now that most teams are hybrid or remote, the default meeting is finally getting questioned.

> The data is clear: teams that moved to async-first report 30% fewer meetings and no drop in output. But async-first does not mean async-only. The founders getting this right keep synchronous time for two things: decisions that need debate and relationships that need face time. Everything else — status updates, code reviews, project briefs — goes async.

> If your calendar has more than 8 hours of meetings this week and you run a team under 30, something is broken. Audit your recurring meetings. Cancel anything that could be a Loom video or a Notion doc. You will not miss them.

### What Changed

- Eliminated every hedge: "it seems like", "could potentially", "arguably", "it might be worth considering." Replaced with direct claims.
- Opened with a strong declarative: "The async-first movement is not a trend. It is a correction."
- Added a specific statistic: "30% fewer meetings and no drop in output."
- Acknowledged the nuance without hedging: "async-first does not mean async-only."
- Ended with a concrete, actionable instruction: "Audit your recurring meetings. Cancel anything that could be a Loom video or a Notion doc."
- Applied the small-team lens: "you run a team under 30."

---

## Example 5: Newsletter Introduction (Dead Opener + Rhythm)

### Before (Generic)

> Welcome to this week's edition of the Founder OS newsletter! It's been a busy week in the tech world, and we've got a lot to cover. In this issue, we'll discuss three major topics: AI pricing changes, the shift to async communication, and a new approach to customer onboarding. Let's dive in!

### After (Founder Voice)

> Three things happened this week that change how you run your business. One saves you money. One saves you time. One fixes the reason 40% of your trial users never come back. None of them require more than an afternoon to implement.

### What Changed

- Cut "Welcome to this week's edition" dead opener. Started with the content.
- Removed "It's been a busy week" (the writer's schedule is irrelevant).
- Replaced the table-of-contents format with intrigue — each topic framed by its benefit, not its label.
- Eliminated "Let's dive in!" filler.
- Added a practical framing hook at the end: "None of them require more than an afternoon to implement."
- Parallel structure creates rhythm: "One saves you money. One saves you time. One fixes..."
- Cut word count from 56 to 41 while increasing reader motivation to continue.

---

## Example 6: Listicle Recovery (Narrative Structure)

### Before (Listicle-Only)

> 5 Tools Every Founder Needs in 2026:
>
> 1. Linear - Great for project management
> 2. Notion - Perfect for documentation
> 3. Loom - Essential for async communication
> 4. Stripe - Best payment processing
> 5. Vercel - Ideal for deployment

### After (Founder Voice)

> The tools conversation in founder circles has gotten stale. Everyone shares the same list. But the tools that actually changed how I work this year are not the ones on most "essential tools" lists — they are the ones that eliminated a recurring weekly task.

> Here is my real stack, ranked by hours saved per week:

> - **Linear** (3 hrs/week saved): Replaced Jira and our Monday grooming ritual. The team actually updates tickets because the UI does not fight them.
> - **Loom** (2.5 hrs/week saved): Killed four recurring meetings. Every status update and code review walkthrough is now a 3-minute video.
> - **Notion** (2 hrs/week saved): Not for documentation — for replacing the 6 different places documentation used to live.

> I left Stripe and Vercel off the list on purpose. They are infrastructure. You need them the way you need electricity — but they do not change how you spend your Tuesday. The tools that matter are the ones that give you time back.

### What Changed

- Wrapped the list in narrative context: why this list, why these tools, what makes this different from every other tools list.
- Added a quantified metric to each item: hours saved per week.
- Included a one-sentence justification per tool explaining the specific behavior change, not just the category.
- Followed the list with synthesis and an opinionated take: "The tools that matter are the ones that give you time back."
- Used the "most people get this wrong" opinion injection variant to frame the list.
- Deliberately excluded expected items and explained why — demonstrates independent thinking.

---

## Example 7: Corporate Jargon Cleanup

### Before (Jargon-Heavy)

> By leveraging best-in-class AI solutions, founders can synergize their workflows to move the needle on key performance indicators. At the end of the day, it's about creating a robust ecosystem that delivers scalable solutions for stakeholders across the organization.

### After (Founder Voice)

> Three AI tools cut our report generation time from 6 hours to 45 minutes. That is not a productivity hack. That is getting a team member back for a full day every week. The tools: Claude for first drafts, Midjourney for charts that do not look like Excel threw up, and a custom Notion integration that pulls the data automatically. Total setup time: one weekend.

### What Changed

- Every piece of jargon replaced with a specific claim: "leveraging best-in-class AI" became three named tools with a measurable outcome.
- "Move the needle on KPIs" became "cut report generation time from 6 hours to 45 minutes."
- "Robust ecosystem" and "scalable solutions" became "Total setup time: one weekend."
- "Stakeholders across the organization" became an implicit audience: the founder and their team.
- Zero jargon. Every sentence contains verifiable information.

---

## Example 8: Passive Voice Correction

### Before (Passive)

> A decision was made to deprecate the legacy API. It was determined that the migration timeline would be set to 90 days. Users were notified via email, and documentation was updated to reflect the changes. It is expected that most integrations will be transitioned by the end of Q2.

### After (Founder Voice)

> Stripe deprecated their legacy API on February 15 and gave everyone 90 days to migrate. They emailed affected users and updated their docs the same day. If you are still on the v1 endpoints, you have until May 15. Most migrations take a weekend. Do not wait until May 14.

### What Changed

- Named the actor in every sentence: "Stripe deprecated", "They emailed", "you have until."
- Replaced "it was determined" and "it is expected" passive constructions with direct statements.
- Added a specific date instead of "end of Q2."
- Ended with a practical recommendation and a touch of humor: "Do not wait until May 14."
- Every sentence has a clear subject performing an action.
